Hey, welcome back, and that was the art of looping, taught to you live. And the question I always get from the audience at this point is, how many loops am I supposed to run, Jordan? Which is the same as saying, basically, when is enough enough? I mean, how many times can I loop around and ask on for the order without getting myself thrown out of someone's office or having them slam the phone down in my ear? And the answer to that is that looping is not just a science, but it's also an art. The science of looping lies in your ability to run loop after loop after loop without running out of intelligent ethical things to say. And the art of looping lies in knowing when you've reached that point of diminishing returns, where you've said just about everything there is to say that's elegant, ethical, and anything else you say will be basically in bad taste and perceived as nothing more than sheer pressure on your part. So there's a few different ways to know when you've reached that point, with the first being, number one, that your prospect starts to stick to the same objection again and again, no matter how many times you try to loop around it and overcome it. In other words, after the second or third time of someone saying that they really need to talk to their wife or check with their partner, whatever the objection might be, if they keep sticking to that, then chances are they really do need to talk to their partner or check with their wife. And conversely, one sure fire way to know that there's definitely still room for more loops is if the person keeps shifting objections with each new loop. In other words, if his first objection was that he wanted to think about it, then after you run a loop on him, which ends with you asking for the order as all loops do, right, and he comes back to you with, I need to check with my wife or another random objection, then you pretty much know there's definitely still room for a few more loops. Now number two, another way you'll know, we've crossed over this threshold into bad taste is by the tonality he uses with his objection. If it's a kind, respectful tonality where he still seems genuinely interested, but he's not just 100% there yet, then there's definitely room for one or two more loops. And conversely, if his objection is laced with that rude, you know, you're wasting my time, pal, tonality, or you can sense that he's just about itching to hang up that phone, and then chances are that you're very close to the point of diminishing returns. And once you've been doing this for a while, one thing you'll notice is that when you ask for the order after your second or third loop, they'll almost start to stutter, as if they're torn in some way between buying and not buying. And what's actually happening there is that when you're running effective loops, you're essentially cutting right to the heart of all of someone's buying concerns. Almost if you're reading their mind, and it starts to freak people out sometimes in a good way. They feel like they've finally found a salesman who understands them, knows where they're coming from, and it's a very liberating feeling for a person. See, when you're running loops, you're basically giving them no choice, but to say yes, unless they want to go against their own core beliefs about buying and fitting in and making good decisions and looking after their own interests in the long term. Essentially you've made this unbelievably airtight case both logically and emotionally, and you've lowered their action threshold to the point where it simply makes sense for them to buy. So if they don't buy, they start to feel conflicted. That's when using the straight line at the highest possible level. Giving the prospect essentially no reasonable basis to say no, because you've already gotten to the point where he says, number one, he loves your product, right? He loves your product. That's the thing since sliced bread. Number two, he trusts and connects with you at a very high level, deeply connected on rapport, right? Number three, he loves the company, knows that the company will stand behind the product, it's a solid company for the long term. It will be an asset to him over the long term, and you continue to loop around and loop around and answer his objections and keep getting back to the heart of the matter, those three tens, and you lower that action threshold by making him play out that good movie, meaning all the wonderful things that will happen if he buys your product, and he sees himself in the future actually using your product, feeling good, feeling smart for having made this decision. Then when you go through the negative movie about what will go wrong if he buys, that's a very short movie with limited downside risk, and of course you have your money back guarantees typically to offset that negative movie, right? So you run this amazing long movie, this very, very short bad movie, and you have him loving the product, loving you, loving your company, then you ask for the order calmly and without pressure. There is simply no reason for someone not to buy, and if he says no, he's like torn there, he starts to feel conflicted. He's like, you know, I want to buy it, I really do, but I just can't buy it because, you know, my mommy told me not to trust a salesperson, or back when I was six, you know, I heard something bad about salespeople, I've been burnt before, you know, but then they say, well, this guy's different. This guy seems like he really understands me, and he told me that there's really not that much that can go wrong. He's lowered my action threshold, they won't use those words, but they're feeling it, and that conflict, and you start driving at that conflict, and you're essentially empowering someone to make a very intelligent, a very good buying decision by building up certainty for the three 10s, right? For your product number one, for you number two, for your company number three, then you lower the action threshold to make them feel comfortable about the long term decisions, so they can really see themselves using the product in the future and feeling really, really good about it. Then you add on a little bit of pain if it's necessary, right? Because some people are in denial, and if we're in denial, we tend not to take action. So yeah, add on the little magic bullet of pain, which lowers his action threshold, then you ask for the order one last time, calmly and elegantly, and bam, just like that, people start to say yes. And I've seen this happen again, and again, and again, I mean, thousands of times, literally, there, someone will say no, no, no, no, no, no, and again, when I say no, I mean, they give you some objection, not a hard no, but objection after objection after objection, all of a sudden, like, okay, I'll do it. Now the question I really have for you there is, when that's happening, it might seem like you're actually not making that much progress here. You're like, wow, I keep asking the guy to buy, and I'm giving him all his wonderful reasons to buy, yet no matter what I say to him, he just won't seem to buy. And yet all of a sudden, at the very end, after 15 or 20 minutes of this, he all of a sudden says, let's do it. You say, what was going on during that time frame? Well, actually, here's the deal. You were making massive, massive progress during that 15 or 20 minute time frame, while you were looping around and building certainty and lowering his action threshold by making the play out a positive movie. What was happening was that all that progress was below the water line. In other words, it wasn't visible, but there was progress going on. So as a salesperson, you have to remember, don't be disheartened with all these objections that are going to come up as you're looping, as you're taking someone down the straight line and building certainty. But I promise you this, if you continue to build certainty and you do it right, you do it elegantly, you do it effectively, and you say intelligent things, and then you'll lower that person's action threshold and add on that little bit of pain, you'll watch the magic start to happen. That's the bottom line. And looping is really the heart of the straight line at the highest possible level. It allows you, as a salesperson, to close pretty much anyone who's closable. And to do it in a way that doesn't piss people off, it doesn't cause you to break rapport when they're saying, let me think about it, and you're saying, oh, you don't need to think about it. That's ridiculous. That's what typically happens. What really makes me laugh about many traditional sales trading programs is they'll teach you to answer objections by actually answering someone's objection. Like, if someone says, let me think about it, they'll tell you three things that you can say why he shouldn't have to think about it. And that's ridiculous, because in fact, when someone says they want to think about it, it's not because they really have to think about it. What they're saying is, I'm not certain yet, Jordan, that it's code words for I'm not certain yet. When someone says I want to speak to my wife, it's code words for I'm not certain yet, Jordan, or I have a very high action threshold, Jordan, make me more certain. Tell me why it's a great decision. Make me feel good about the future. That's what people are saying when they're giving you these objections. And looping is this scientific, almost literally a breakthrough. And I've seen this really breakthrough, terrible salespeople who simply cannot close a damn door. I teach them this art of looping and the science of looping. And just like that, boom, their closing rate goes through the roof.
